solutions.solution:
Which of the following means that "A is the grandmother of B"? 1) A + B × C - D ÷ F 2) A × C - D + B ÷ F 3) A - B × C + D ÷ E 4) A × C + B + D ÷ F 5) None of these

Here, in this question, since A is to be the grandmother of B, A must be a female and B a male/female. Also, in coded words in (1) and (2) mean that A is a male. Because in coded statement a symbol + or × indicates male, therefore, in these two choices we don't need to look any further as A is straightaway eliminate them.

So, A is the following means that "X is the grandson of Y"?
1) Y × A → B ÷ C × Y 2) X + A → B ÷ C × Y
3) Y × B + C × Y 4) X A B B + C × Y

Here, in this question, if X is to be the grandson of Y, X must be a male. But there in the choices (1), (2) and (4) make X out to be a female. For example, in Ex. 2, there's no symbol A × means A is a daughter (hence a female) of A. Therefore, we can straightaway eliminate choices (1), (2) and (4).

Which of the following means that "B is the grandfather of E"?
1) A ÷ B × C ÷ D ÷ E 2) A × B ÷ C ÷ D ÷ E
3) A ÷ B × C × D ÷ E 4) A ÷ B ÷ C ÷ D ÷ E
5) None of these

Here, in this question, since B is to be the grandfather of E, B must be a male. But choice (2), (3) and (4) are quickly rejected as these B turns out to be female in the first glance. For example, in (2): A × B means B is the daughter (hence a female) of A; in (3) A ÷ B means B is the sister (hence a female) of A. Hence, (2), (3), (4) are quickly eliminated.

Note: Note that Ex. 3 was a case where the relationships were coded in the backward type format. And in this case we rejected the wrong choices (2), (3) and (4) because there the code that indicated the wrong sex of B appeared before B.

[See A ~ B in (2), A ÷ B in (3) and (4)]. On the other hand, in Ex. 1 and Ex. 2, the relationships were coded in the forward type format. And in that case we rejected the wrong choices because there are codes that indicated the wrong sex of A and X appeared after A and X. For example, [See A + B in (1) and A × C in (2) of Ex. 1, X + A in (1) and (2) and X in (4) of Ex. 2]. This gives us our brief and quick short-cut technique.
